Just now

--

What is Node.js?
Originally, JavaScript was designed to run only inside a web browser. To change this, a developer named Ryan Dahl took Google Chrome’s V8 JavaScript engine and combined it with C++ to create Node.js. This environment allows developers to run JavaScript on the server side. To execute a file, you simply type node filename.js in your terminal.
NPM and Browser Limitations
Node Package Manager (npm) comes pre-installed with Node.js and is used to manage third-party packages. When you run npm init in your terminal, it initializes a new project and generates a package.json file to keep track of your dependencies.
Note: Because Node.js runs on a server and not in a browser, browser-specific features like the DOM and window.alert() will not work.
Modular Programming
Node.js relies heavily on modular programming — the practice of breaking a large codebase down into smaller, reusable files (modules) to make development easier. While Node has many built-in modules, you will often create your own.
Exporting and Importing
By default, functions you write inside a file (like math.js) are private. To use them in another file, you must export them using module.exports or exports. If you have more than one function, you can group them into a single object and export them all at once.
To use that module in your main file (like index.js), you import it using the require() function. It is important to include ./ (e.g., require('./math')) so Node.js knows to look for the file in your current directory. If you leave out the ./, Node will search for an installed npm package instead.
File Handling and Creation
File handling means doing operations on a file, like creating or reading it. All of these things cannot be done in normal JavaScript but in Node.js, we have a special fs module to read, write, delete, and append files..
Creating Files
To create a file we use fs.writeFileSync("./filename.txt", "hello world"). For async, we use fs.writeFile("./filename.txt", "hello world async", (err)=>{}).For backend development, we should have clarity between blocking and non-blocking functions, also called sync and async.
Reading Files
Now, to read a file, we use the function const result = fs.readFileSync("./filename.txt", "utf-8") to show all the data from the file in the terminal. Sync functions return the result directly, but async functions do not return a result; they always expect a callback.
Appending and Log Files
To append a file, we use fs.appendFileSync("./text.txt", "something to do or append, you can put dates"). This does not overwrite the file; it just appends the data. We can use this to make a log file where we can store the IP address of a person, what they have searched, and other details like the date and time.
Additional File Operations
There are also functions like fs.statSync. We have many functions like this in the module.
